sanskrit:
अर्जुन उवाच
ज्यायसी चेत्कर्मणस्ते मता बुद्धिर्जनार्दन।
तत्किं कर्मणि घोरे मां नियोजयसि केशव।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvāca
jyāyasī cet karmaṇas te matā buddhir janārdana
tat kiṃ karmaṇi ghore māṃ niyojayasi keśava
literal_meaning: Arjuna said: O Janārdana, if You hold that wisdom is superior to action, why then do You urge me into this dreadful action, O Keśava?
contextual_meaning: Arjuna attempts to resolve cognitive dissonance by using Kṛṣṇa’s own teaching as a reason to avoid battle, revealing the ego’s subtle trick of spiritual bypassing.
psychological_state: Approach-avoidance conflict: he both desires the moral high ground and fears the consequences of action, so he reframes avoidance as spiritual superiority.
trigger_scenario: A manager who has been told that ‘mindfulness is better than burnout’ uses this teaching to dodge a difficult but necessary layoff conversation, asking HR: ‘If mindfulness is superior, why push me into this horrible task?’
anchor_text: Wisdom and action unite when you serve without clinging.
